articles.title: Ceasefire Extended: Israel and Lebanon Give Peace Three More Weeks
articles.summary: Imagine you're in a huge argument with a neighbor, and you both agree to stop yelling for a while—then you decide to keep quiet for a few more weeks. That's what Israel and Lebanon just did! President Trump announced they agreed to extend their ceasefire (a pause in fighting) by three weeks. The first 10-day ceasefire was about to expire, but after a White House meeting, the ambassadors said yes to more time. The goal is to stop attacks, pull Israeli troops out of Lebanon, free prisoners, and start rebuilding. But a group called Hezbollah says it won't follow the agreement, and there have already been violations—including the killing of a journalist. Many hope this extension will lead to lasting…

articles.title: Mind Games at the White House: Mentalist Oz Pearlman to Perform at Correspondents' Dinner
articles.summary: For over a century, the White House Correspondents' Dinner has featured comedians roasting politicians. But this year, something completely different is happening: a mentalist named Oz Pearlman will be the headliner. Mentalists are like magicians who seem to read minds—guessing PIN numbers, secrets, and thoughts. Pearlman went viral for his tricks on TV and social media. President Trump plans to attend, and Pearlman hints that Trump will be part of the act. "Reading Donald Trump's mind is arguably the most impressive thing you could ever do," he says. It's a bold move that breaks tradition, but everyone's curious to see if he can pull off a jaw-dropping moment.

articles.title: Suing Their Own Boss: Emory Professors Take University to Court Over Protest Arrests
articles.summary: Three tenured professors at Emory University are suing their own employer over arrests during a 2024 pro-Palestine protest. The professors—Noelle McAfee, Emilio Del Valle-Escalante, and Caroline Fohlin—say the university broke its own free-speech policies by calling in police and state troopers to disband a tent protest. One professor was thrown to the ground, suffering a concussion and spine injury. Another was charged just for yelling "Stop!" at an officer. None were convicted. They want the university to repay their legal fees and pay damages. The university calls the lawsuit "without merit." This case highlights the ongoing debate about free speech and protest on college campuses.
